summary: DHH endorses John McWhorter's book 'Woke Racism' and its central thesis that people should refuse to be intimidated by baseless accusations of racism or white supremacy. He uses his own experience receiving such accusations after writing about DEI as a case study. DHH argues that the tactic of labeling dissenters as racists relies on shock value and 'transitive guilt' — where the accusation extends to anyone associated with the accused. He contends that these tactics have lost much of their power since 2020 through overuse, and that standing firm against them accelerates their decline.
key_points: McWhorter frames contemporary anti-racism activism as a quasi-religious movement ('The New Religion') that enforces orthodoxy through shaming
The tactic of 'transitive guilt' — extending accusations to anyone associated with the target — is designed to isolate dissenters and multiply the cost of speaking out
The shock value of being called a racist or white supremacist diminishes rapidly with repeated baseless use, undermining the tactic's effectiveness
DHH argues that the power of these social shaming tactics has weakened significantly since their peak around 2020
Collective refusal to submit to ideological shaming is presented as the mechanism that will force a recalibration
money_quotes: There is room in this society for speaking the truth and living to tell about it.
After the shock of being accused without cause of being a 'racist' or a 'white supremacist' once, it just doesn't have the same effect the next time.
The shock value rapidly diminishes exactly because it is such a serious charge. One that can't be sustained if the accusation isn't plugged into reality.
Transitive guilt is a terrifying tactic the first time you encounter it.
You're not only required to hold your own line, you also have to hold it for everyone else, or you will have brought a pox upon their houses too.
By refusing to bow to these tactics, they will inevitably lose their terror.
What a year or two ago might have been A Really Big Deal is now barely a twirl in a teapot.
key_insight: Baseless accusations of racism lose their power through overuse and collective refusal to be intimidated, and the declining effectiveness of these shaming tactics since 2020 proves that standing firm works.
